title: Memory Wall is not gone: A Critical Outlook on Memory Architecture in Digital Neuromorphic Computing
summary: The rapid advancement of neuromorphic technology aims to address the memory wall challenge inherent in conventional von Neumann architectures. This paper critically examines current digital neuromorphic processors and their strategies to mitigate this bottleneck. While designed to bring computation closer to memory through distributed architectures, our findings indicate that on-chip memory systems, including SRAM and emerging technologies like STT-MRAM, have become significant consumers of area and energy, leading to a new memory wall. Through an analysis of energy and area efficiency in various memory technologies, we argue that without a re-evaluation of memory organization, digital neuromorphic processors may struggle to compete effectively in edge and embedded applications. We conclude with potential pathways for future research to overcome the limitations of on-chip memory in neuromorphic systems.

AI.tldr: Digital neuromorphic processors face a new memory wall despite distributed architectures, as on-chip memory (SRAM, STT-MRAM) consumes excessive area/energy, hindering edge/embedded competitiveness.
AI.motivation: To address the memory wall problem in von Neumann architectures through neuromorphic computing, but discovering that current digital neuromorphic processors create their own memory bottleneck through inefficient on-chip memory systems.
AI.method: Critical examination of current digital neuromorphic processors and their memory strategies, with analysis of energy and area efficiency across various memory technologies (SRAM, STT-MRAM, etc.).
AI.result: On-chip memory systems in digital neuromorphic processors have become significant consumers of area and energy, creating a new memory wall that undermines their advantages for edge/embedded applications.
AI.conclusion: Digital neuromorphic processors need memory organization re-evaluation to compete effectively in edge/embedded applications, with proposed research pathways to overcome on-chip memory limitations.

title: From Indiscriminate to Targeted: Efficient RTL Verification via Functionally Key Signal-Driven LLM Assertion Generation
summary: Functional verification has become the most time-consuming phase in IC development, and Assertion-Based Verification (ABV) is key to reducing debugging time. However, existing LLM-based assertion generation methods typically pursue indiscriminate verification, aiming for maximal coverage without considering signal criticality, whereas industrial practice demands maximizing coverage with minimal verification cost. Consequently, identifying signals that have the greatest impact on design functionality and error propagation-enabling a shift from indiscriminate to targeted verification-remains a key challenge. To address this, we propose AgileAssert, a key signal-driven assertion generation framework that constructs RTL semantic graphs and identifies the top-K critical signals via a hybrid scoring and selection mechanism, followed by structure-aware RTL slicing to provide the LLM with precise targets and contextual information, thereby guiding LLMs to generate tightly constrained targeted assertions for efficient verification. Evaluated on block- and CPU-level designs, with an average 66.68% reduction in assertions, our approach outperforms three existing SOTA methods, and significantly improving coverage metrics while reducing input token consumption by 64%. In mutation testing, when our approach surpasses existing methods in error detection rate, the average number of assertions used decreases by 72.74%.

AI.tldr: AgileAssert is a targeted assertion generation framework that identifies critical signals and generates focused assertions, reducing verification cost while maintaining coverage.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based assertion generation methods pursue indiscriminate verification with maximal coverage, but industrial practice requires maximizing coverage with minimal verification cost. The key challenge is identifying signals with greatest impact on design functionality and error propagation to shift from indiscriminate to targeted verification.
AI.method: Constructs RTL semantic graphs, identifies top-K critical signals via hybrid scoring and selection mechanism, performs structure-aware RTL slicing to provide LLMs with precise targets and contextual information, guiding LLMs to generate tightly constrained targeted assertions.
AI.result: Evaluated on block- and CPU-level designs: average 66.68% reduction in assertions, outperforms three existing SOTA methods, significantly improves coverage metrics while reducing input token consumption by 64%. In mutation testing, surpasses existing methods in error detection rate with average 72.74% fewer assertions.
AI.conclusion: AgileAssert enables targeted verification by focusing on critical signals, achieving efficient verification with substantially fewer assertions while maintaining or improving coverage and error detection capabilities.

title: DRIFT: Harnessing Inherent Fault Tolerance for Efficient and Reliable Diffusion Model Inference
summary: Diffusion model deployment has been suffering from high energy consumption and inference latency despite its superior performance in visual generation tasks. Dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS) offers a promising solution to exploit the potential of the underlying accelerators. However, existing approaches often lead to either limited efficiency gains or degraded output quality because they overlook the inherent fault tolerance of the diffusion model. Therefore, in this paper, we propose DRIFT, a novel algorithmarchitecture co-optimization framework that harnesses the fault tolerance for efficient and reliable diffusion model inference. We first perform a comprehensive resilience analysis on representative diffusion models. Building on these observations, we introduce a fine-grained, resilience-aware DVFS strategy that selectively protects error-sensitive network blocks and timesteps, and a rollback algorithm-based fault tolerance (ABFT) mechanism that adaptively corrects only critical errors by reverting to previous timesteps. We further optimize offloading intervals and reorganize data layouts to reduce memory overhead. Experiments across diverse models and datasets show that DRIFT can achieve on average 36% energy savings through voltage underscaling or 1.7x speedup via overclocking while maintaining generation quality.

AI.tldr: DRIFT is an algorithm-architecture co-optimization framework that leverages diffusion models' fault tolerance to enable efficient DVFS (dynamic voltage/frequency scaling) while maintaining output quality, achieving 36% energy savings or 1.7x speedup.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models suffer from high energy consumption and inference latency despite superior generation quality. Existing DVFS approaches have limited efficiency gains or degrade output quality because they overlook diffusion models' inherent fault tolerance.
AI.method: 1) Comprehensive resilience analysis on diffusion models; 2) Fine-grained, resilience-aware DVFS strategy protecting error-sensitive blocks/timesteps; 3) Rollback algorithm-based fault tolerance (ABFT) mechanism adaptively correcting critical errors by reverting to previous timesteps; 4) Optimizing offloading intervals and reorganizing data layouts to reduce memory overhead.
AI.result: DRIFT achieves average 36% energy savings through voltage underscaling or 1.7x speedup via overclocking while maintaining generation quality across diverse models and datasets.
AI.conclusion: DRIFT successfully exploits diffusion models' fault tolerance through algorithm-architecture co-optimization, enabling efficient DVFS deployment without compromising output quality, addressing the energy-latency challenges in diffusion model inference.

title: Sensor Placement for Tsunami Early Warning via Large-Scale Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design
summary: Real-time tsunami early warning relies on distributed sensor networks to infer seismic sources and seafloor motion. Optimizing these networks via Bayesian optimal experimental design (OED) is exceptionally challenging for systems governed by hyperbolic partial differential equations, which lack the spectral decay required by standard low-rank approximations. We present a scalable Bayesian OED framework for linear time-invariant systems. By reformulating the inverse problem in the data space, we transform OED into dense matrix subset selection. We propose a multi-GPU, Schur-complement-update-based, greedy algorithm that solves the OED problem using a pipelined approach that fully overlaps I/O with GPU computations. Our framework achieves near-perfect weak and strong scaling across hundreds of GPUs on Perlmutter and Frontier. Applied to the 2025 Gordon Bell Prize-winning digital twin for tsunami forecasting in the Cascadia Subduction Zone, we optimize a 175-sensor network, minimizing the uncertainty of a parameter field with over one billion degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: Scalable Bayesian optimal experimental design framework for tsunami early warning networks using GPU-accelerated greedy algorithms to optimize sensor placement in hyperbolic PDE systems.
AI.motivation: Real-time tsunami early warning requires optimized sensor networks, but Bayesian optimal experimental design for hyperbolic PDE systems is challenging due to lack of spectral decay needed for standard low-rank approximations.
AI.method: Reformulate inverse problem in data space to transform OED into dense matrix subset selection; develop multi-GPU, Schur-complement-update-based greedy algorithm with pipelined approach that overlaps I/O with GPU computations.
AI.result: Achieves near-perfect weak and strong scaling across hundreds of GPUs on Perlmutter and Frontier; optimizes 175-sensor network for tsunami forecasting digital twin with over 1 billion degrees of freedom parameter field.
AI.conclusion: Presents scalable Bayesian OED framework for linear time-invariant systems that enables optimization of large-scale sensor networks for real-time tsunami early warning systems.

title: qPRO-AQFP: Post-Routing Optimization of AQFP Circuits with Delay Line Clocking
summary: Adiabatic Quantum-Flux-Parametron (AQFP) logic is an ultra-low-power superconducting logic family with energy consumption approaching the Shannon limit, making it attractive for quantum computing control and cryogenic computing systems. Traditional AQFP designs face significant physical design challenges due to strict gate-level clocking requirements and limited interconnect lengths, leading to substantial buffer overhead and difficult timing closure. Recently, delay-line clocking of AQFP has been proposed to improve timing margins and reduce latency by enabling more flexible clock scheduling. However, prior work has primarily focused on placement and latency minimization, while relying on fixed timing parameters that do not capture the frequency dependence of AQFP setup and hold constraints. To address this limitation, we propose a frequency-aware post-routing optimization framework that jointly optimizes clock period, latency, and timing slack under user-specified weighting. Experimental results across common benchmarks achieve 100% post-routing timing closure across a range of performance--latency--slack trade-offs. Our approach also automates phase-skipping, reducing path-balancing buffer insertion by 34% on average while only reducing operating frequency by 4%.

AI.tldr: A frequency-aware post-routing optimization framework for AQFP logic that jointly optimizes clock period, latency, and timing slack, achieving 100% timing closure with reduced buffer overhead.
AI.motivation: Traditional AQFP designs face physical design challenges due to strict gate-level clocking and limited interconnect lengths, requiring substantial buffer overhead and struggling with timing closure. Existing delay-line clocking approaches use fixed timing parameters that don't capture frequency-dependent setup/hold constraints.
AI.method: Proposed a frequency-aware post-routing optimization framework that jointly optimizes clock period, latency, and timing slack under user-specified weighting. The approach automates phase-skipping and considers frequency-dependent timing constraints.
AI.result: Achieved 100% post-routing timing closure across benchmarks with various performance-latency-slack trade-offs. Reduced path-balancing buffer insertion by 34% on average while only reducing operating frequency by 4%.
AI.conclusion: The frequency-aware optimization framework effectively addresses timing closure challenges in AQFP logic, enabling better trade-offs between performance, latency, and timing slack while significantly reducing buffer overhead.

title: EdgeFlow: Fast Cold Starts for LLMs on Mobile Devices
summary: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on mobile devices is an emerging trend to enable data privacy and offline accessibility of LLM applications. Modern mobile neural processing units (NPUs) make such deployment increasingly feasible. However, existing mobile LLM inference frameworks suffer from high start-up latency due to their inevitable cold starts, i.e., launching LLM inferences when the model is not hosted in device memory. In this paper, we identify the key bottleneck of mobile LLM cold starts as the waste of flash bandwidth on unimportant model parameters. We design EdgeFlow, a mobile LLM inference framework that mitigates the cold start issue by adaptively adjusting the precisions of LLM parameters. Specifically, EdgeFlow leverages 1) an NPU-aware adaptive quantization algorithm that assigns different precisions to weights in a finer granularity according to their importance and NPU constraints, 2) an SIMD-friendly packing format that accelerates the transformation of various-precision weights into fixed-sized NPU-native data types, and 3) a synergistic granular pipeline that coordinates CPU and NPU computation in a fine-grained and dynamic manner. Experimental results show that EdgeFlow reduces cold-start latency by up to 4.07x compared with three state-of-the-art mobile LLM inference frameworks, i.e., llama.cpp, MNN, and llm.npu, under comparable model accuracy.

AI.tldr: EdgeFlow reduces mobile LLM cold-start latency by 4.07x through adaptive quantization, SIMD-friendly packing, and granular CPU-NPU coordination.
AI.motivation: Mobile LLM deployment faces high cold-start latency due to flash bandwidth waste on unimportant parameters when models aren't in device memory, despite modern NPUs making deployment feasible.
AI.method: EdgeFlow uses: 1) NPU-aware adaptive quantization assigning different precisions to weights based on importance and NPU constraints, 2) SIMD-friendly packing format for efficient transformation of various-precision weights to NPU-native types, and 3) synergistic granular pipeline coordinating CPU and NPU computation dynamically.
AI.result: EdgeFlow reduces cold-start latency by up to 4.07x compared to state-of-the-art frameworks (llama.cpp, MNN, llm.npu) while maintaining comparable model accuracy.
AI.conclusion: EdgeFlow effectively mitigates mobile LLM cold-start issues through adaptive precision adjustment and optimized system coordination, enabling more efficient mobile LLM deployment with privacy and offline benefits.

title: MATCHA: Efficient Deployment of Deep Neural Networks on Multi-Accelerator Heterogeneous Edge SoCs
comment: Accepted at the 63rd ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC26)
summary: Deploying DNNs on System-on-Chips (SoC) with multiple heterogeneous acceleration engines is challenging, and the majority of deployment frameworks cannot fully exploit heterogeneity. We present MATCHA, a unified DNN deployment framework that generates highly concurrent schedules for parallel, heterogeneous accelerators and uses constraint programming to optimize L3/L2 memory allocation and scheduling. Using pattern matching, tiling, and mapping across individual HW units enables parallel execution and high accelerator utilization. On the MLPerf Tiny benchmark, using a SoC with two heterogeneous accelerators, MATCHA improves accelerator utilization and reduces inference latency by up to 35% with respect to the the state-of-the-art MATCH compiler.

AI.tldr: MATCHA is a DNN deployment framework for heterogeneous SoCs that uses constraint programming to optimize memory allocation and scheduling, achieving up to 35% lower latency than state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: Most deployment frameworks cannot fully exploit heterogeneity in SoCs with multiple heterogeneous acceleration engines, making DNN deployment challenging and inefficient.
AI.method: MATCHA uses constraint programming to optimize L3/L2 memory allocation and scheduling, combined with pattern matching, tiling, and mapping across individual hardware units to enable parallel execution.
AI.result: On MLPerf Tiny benchmark with a SoC containing two heterogeneous accelerators, MATCHA improves accelerator utilization and reduces inference latency by up to 35% compared to the state-of-the-art MATCH compiler.
AI.conclusion: MATCHA provides an effective unified framework for deploying DNNs on heterogeneous SoCs by generating highly concurrent schedules and optimizing memory allocation, significantly outperforming existing solutions.

title: Finding Nemo-Nemo: CFT DAG-based Consensus in the WAN
summary: This paper introduces Nemo-Nemo, a practical crash-fault tolerant (CFT) consensus protocol designed to outperform existing protocols in wide-area networks by bridging design principles from the CFT and Byzantine-fault tolerant (BFT) worlds. By structuring command propagation through a causally ordered DAG, Nemo-Nemo allows all consensus replicas to propose commands with a naturally self-regulating communication regime. By exploiting multi-leader architecture, Nemo-Nemo avoids the performance bottleneck inherent to single-leader protocols. By separating command dissemination from consensus logic, Nemo-Nemo handles challenging network conditions even when consensus commits are stalled. Moreover, leader proposals that miss a deadline are never dropped, but deterministically deferred and executed later, preserving throughput under transient network delays. And by enabling Nemo-Nemo to commit on a DAG in just two network hops, it matches the latency of existing CFT systems, while achieving significantly higher throughput. The result is a robust, deployable system: the first DAG-based CFT consensus protocol proven to exceed state-of-the-art wide-area network performance in both speed and resilience.

AI.tldr: Nemo-Nemo is a crash-fault tolerant consensus protocol that combines CFT and BFT design principles, using a causally ordered DAG structure and multi-leader architecture to achieve high throughput and low latency in wide-area networks.
AI.motivation: To create a practical crash-fault tolerant consensus protocol that outperforms existing protocols in wide-area networks by bridging design principles from both CFT and BFT worlds, overcoming performance bottlenecks and handling challenging network conditions.
AI.method: Uses a causally ordered DAG structure for command propagation, multi-leader architecture to avoid single-leader bottlenecks, separates command dissemination from consensus logic, defers rather than drops late leader proposals, and commits on DAG in just two network hops.
AI.result: Achieves significantly higher throughput than existing CFT systems while matching their latency, becoming the first DAG-based CFT consensus protocol proven to exceed state-of-the-art wide-area network performance in both speed and resilience.
AI.conclusion: Nemo-Nemo represents a robust, deployable system that successfully bridges CFT and BFT design principles to create a high-performance consensus protocol suitable for wide-area network deployments with superior throughput and resilience characteristics.

title: A Physically-Informed Subgraph Isomorphism Approach to Molecular Docking Using Quantum Annealers
summary: Molecular docking is a crucial step in the development of new drugs as it guides the positioning of a small molecule (ligand) within the pocket of a target protein. In the literature, a feasibility study explored the potential of D-Wave quantum annealers for purely geometric molecular docking, neglecting physicochemical interactions between the protein and the ligand and focusing solely on their simplified geometries. To achieve this, the ligands were represented as graphs incorporating their geometric properties and then mapped onto a grid that discretized the three-dimensional space of the protein pocket. The quality of the ligand pose on the protein pocket was evaluated through the isomorphism between the ligand graph and the spatial grid. This paper builds on the previous study by introducing physicochemical interactions between the protein-ligand pair into the QUBO problem to improve the accuracy of the docking results. This paper presents a novel QUBO formulation that includes Coulomb and van der Waals forces, together with components representing H-bond and hydrophobic interactions. We integrate these physical interactions as corrective terms to the previous purely geometric QUBO formulation, and provide experimental results using the D-Wave quantum annealers to demonstrate their impact on the accuracy of the docking results.

AI.tldr: This paper extends previous quantum annealing work on molecular docking by adding physicochemical interactions (Coulomb, van der Waals, H-bond, hydrophobic) to the QUBO formulation, improving docking accuracy beyond purely geometric approaches.
AI.motivation: Previous work on quantum annealing for molecular docking only considered geometric properties, neglecting important physicochemical interactions between proteins and ligands that are crucial for accurate drug development.
AI.method: The authors developed a novel QUBO formulation that incorporates Coulomb and van der Waals forces, plus H-bond and hydrophobic interactions as corrective terms to the previous purely geometric formulation, then tested it using D-Wave quantum annealers.
AI.result: Experimental results using D-Wave quantum annealers demonstrate that including physicochemical interactions improves the accuracy of molecular docking results compared to the previous purely geometric approach.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating physicochemical interactions into quantum annealing-based molecular docking significantly enhances accuracy, making quantum computing approaches more viable for practical drug development applications.

title: Watt Counts: Energy-Aware Benchmark for Sustainable LLM Inference on Heterogeneous GPU Architectures
summary: While the large energy consumption of Large Language Models (LLMs) is recognized by the community, system operators lack guidance for energy-efficient LLM inference deployments that leverage energy trade-offs of heterogeneous hardware due to a lack of energy-aware benchmarks and data. In this work we address this gap with Watt Counts: the largest open-access dataset of energy consumption of LLMs, with over 5,000 experiments for 50 LLMs across 10 NVIDIA Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) in batch and server scenarios along with a reproducible, open-source benchmark that enables community submissions to expand this dataset. Leveraging this dataset, we conduct a system-level study of LLM inference across heterogeneous GPU architectures and show that GPU selection is crucial for energy efficiency outcomes and that optimal hardware choices vary significantly across models and deployment scenarios, demonstrating the critical importance of hardware-aware deployment in heterogeneous LLM systems. Guided by our data and insights, we show that practitioners can reduce energy consumption by up to 70% in server scenarios with negligible impact on user experience, and by up to 20% in batch scenarios.

AI.tldr: Watt Counts provides the largest open-access dataset of LLM energy consumption across heterogeneous GPUs, enabling hardware-aware deployment decisions that can reduce energy consumption by up to 70% in server scenarios.
AI.motivation: System operators lack guidance for energy-efficient LLM inference deployments due to missing energy-aware benchmarks and data on heterogeneous hardware trade-offs.
AI.method: Created Watt Counts: largest open-access dataset with 5,000+ experiments for 50 LLMs across 10 NVIDIA GPUs in batch/server scenarios, plus reproducible benchmark for community submissions.
AI.result: GPU selection is crucial for energy efficiency; optimal hardware varies significantly across models and scenarios. Practitioners can reduce energy by up to 70% in server scenarios and 20% in batch scenarios with negligible user impact.
AI.conclusion: Hardware-aware deployment in heterogeneous LLM systems is critical for energy efficiency, and the Watt Counts dataset enables data-driven decisions to achieve substantial energy savings.

title: TensorHub: Scalable and Elastic Weight Transfer for LLM RL Training
summary:
Modern LLM reinforcement learning (RL) workloads require a highly efficient weight transfer system to scale training across heterogeneous computational resources. However, existing weight transfer approaches either fail to provide flexibility for dynamically scaling clusters or incur fundamental data movement overhead, resulting in poor performance.
  We introduce Reference-Oriented Storage (ROS), a new storage abstraction for RL weight transfer that exploits the highly replicated model weights in place. ROS presents the illusion that certain versions of the model weights are stored and can be fetched on demand. Underneath, ROS does not physically store any copies of the weights; instead, it tracks the workers that hold these weights on GPUs for inference. Upon request, ROS directly uses them to serve reads. We build TensorHub, a production-quality system that extends the ROS idea with topology-optimized transfer, strong consistency, and fault tolerance. Evaluation shows that TensorHub fully saturates RDMA bandwidth and adapts to three distinct rollout workloads with minimal engineering effort. Specifically, TensorHub reduces total GPU stall time by up to 6.7x for standalone rollouts, accelerates weight update for elastic rollout by 4.8x, and cuts cross-datacenter rollout stall time by 19x. TensorHub has been deployed in production to support cutting-edge RL training.

AI.tldr: TensorHub introduces Reference-Oriented Storage (ROS) for efficient LLM reinforcement learning weight transfer, eliminating physical weight copies by tracking GPU-held weights and serving reads directly from inference workers.
AI.motivation: Modern LLM RL workloads need efficient weight transfer across heterogeneous resources, but existing approaches lack flexibility for dynamic scaling or incur data movement overhead, leading to poor performance.
AI.method: Introduces Reference-Oriented Storage (ROS) abstraction that tracks workers holding model weights on GPUs for inference, serving reads directly from them without physical storage. TensorHub extends ROS with topology-optimized transfer, strong consistency, and fault tolerance.
AI.result: TensorHub fully saturates RDMA bandwidth, adapts to three rollout workloads with minimal engineering effort: reduces GPU stall time by up to 6.7x for standalone rollouts, accelerates weight update for elastic rollout by 4.8x, and cuts cross-datacenter rollout stall time by 19x.
AI.conclusion: TensorHub with ROS provides an efficient weight transfer solution for LLM RL training, has been deployed in production to support cutting-edge RL training by eliminating physical weight copies and optimizing data movement.

title: Sustaining Exascale Performance: Lessons from HPL and HPL-MxP on Aurora
summary: Sustaining exascale performance in production requires engineering choices and operational practices that emerge only under real deployment constraints and demand coordination across system layers. This paper reports experience from three successive campaigns running HPL and HPL-MxP on Aurora, an Intel-based exascale system featuring the first large-scale deployment of Intel discrete GPUs, CPU-attached network interfaces, and the largest production Slingshot-11 interconnect. Aurora progressed from 0.585EF/s on 5,439 nodes to 1.01EF/s on 9,234 nodes in FP64 HPL, while HPL-MxP reached 11.64EF/s, an 11.5x speedup over FP64 enabled by mixed-precision arithmetic and Intel AMX acceleration. We identify and classify by role at production scale the system-level choices that sustained these results, including deterministic locality-aware resource mapping, explicit CPU-GPU pipelining, mixed-precision orchestration, and a hybrid P2P/collective resilience strategy introduced after synchronization stalls at scale. While some observations are Aurora-specific, the broader lessons are likely to apply to tightly coupled heterogeneous systems at extreme scale.

AI.tldr: Aurora exascale system achieved 1.01EF/s on HPL and 11.64EF/s on HPL-MxP through production-scale optimizations including deterministic resource mapping, CPU-GPU pipelining, mixed-precision orchestration, and hybrid resilience strategies.
AI.motivation: To understand the engineering choices and operational practices needed to sustain exascale performance in production environments, particularly for tightly coupled heterogeneous systems at extreme scale.
AI.method: Three successive campaigns running HPL and HPL-MxP on Aurora exascale system, analyzing system-level optimizations including deterministic locality-aware resource mapping, explicit CPU-GPU pipelining, mixed-precision orchestration, and hybrid P2P/collective resilience strategies.
AI.result: Aurora progressed from 0.585EF/s on 5,439 nodes to 1.01EF/s on 9,234 nodes in FP64 HPL, while HPL-MxP reached 11.64EF/s (11.5x speedup over FP64) enabled by mixed-precision arithmetic and Intel AMX acceleration.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies system-level choices that sustained exascale performance, with broader lessons applicable to tightly coupled heterogeneous systems at extreme scale, despite some Aurora-specific observations.
